content.text: Choosing between prompting, RAG, and fine-tuning is as much a financial decision as a technical one. Prompting has zero upfront cost but the highest per-request spend; fine-tuning demands several thousand dollars of one-time investment then saves money at high volume; RAG sits in between with fixed monthly infrastructure and reduced per-token costs. Getting the numbers right before committing prevents expensive surprises.

attrs.alt: Break-even line chart comparing prompting, RAG, and fine-tuning costs across request volume, with TCO comparison table and reference pricing

content.text: Every approach prices at the token level. The cost per request under pure prompting is:
are the provider's input/output prices per token, and
are the mean token counts. With a fine-tuned model the per-request cost drops because the system prompt is shorter (behaviour is baked into weights) and a smaller model may be sufficient:

attrs.latex: C_{\text{FT}} = p_{\text{in}}^{\prime} \cdot T_{\text{in}}^{\prime} + p_{\text{out}}^{\prime} \cdot T_{\text{out}}^{\prime} \quad (p^{\prime} < p,\; T_{\text{in}}^{\prime} < T_{\text{in}})

content.text: RAG adds a fixed monthly infrastructure term
(vector database, embedding pipeline) amortised over the total request volume
Fine-tuning breaks even against prompting when its amortised per-request saving pays off the one-time training and engineering investment

With typical numbers (Sonnet at $3/$15 per million tokens, a 600-token system prompt, 400 completion tokens, versus a fine-tuned Haiku at $0.80/$4 with a 50-token prompt, plus $3 k engineering), the break-even falls around 730 k requests. The following code makes this concrete and lets you plug in your own numbers:

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class TokenPricing:
    """Per-token prices in USD."""
    input_per_million: float
    output_per_million: float

    def cost(self, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float:
        return (
            self.input_per_million * input_tokens / 1_000_000
            + self.output_per_million * output_tokens / 1_000_000
        )


# ── Reference prices (mid-2025) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
SONNET   = TokenPricing(input_per_million=3.00,  output_per_million=15.00)
HAIKU    = TokenPricing(input_per_million=0.80,  output_per_million=4.00)
GPT4O_MINI_FT = TokenPricing(input_per_million=0.30, output_per_million=1.20)


def prompting_cost_per_request(
    pricing: TokenPricing,
    system_tokens: int,
    user_tokens: int,
    completion_tokens: int,
) -> float:
    return pricing.cost(system_tokens + user_tokens, completion_tokens)


def rag_cost_per_request(
    pricing: TokenPricing,
    user_tokens: int,
    retrieved_tokens: int,
    completion_tokens: int,
    monthly_infra_usd: float,
    monthly_requests: int,
) -> float:
    infra_per_request = monthly_infra_usd / monthly_requests
    token_cost = pricing.cost(user_tokens + retrieved_tokens, completion_tokens)
    return infra_per_request + token_cost


def break_even_requests(
    cost_per_request_before: float,
    cost_per_request_after: float,
    one_time_investment_usd: float,
) -> float:
    """Number of requests at which fine-tuning pays off."""
    saving = cost_per_request_before - cost_per_request_after
    if saving <= 0:
        return float("inf")
    return one_time_investment_usd / saving


# ── Example calculation ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Baseline: Claude Sonnet with a 600-token system prompt
    c_prompt = prompting_cost_per_request(
        SONNET,
        system_tokens=600,
        user_tokens=200,
        completion_tokens=400,
    )

    # Fine-tuned: Haiku with behaviour baked in (50-token prompt)
    c_ft = prompting_cost_per_request(
        HAIKU,
        system_tokens=50,
        user_tokens=200,
        completion_tokens=400,
    )

    # RAG baseline: Sonnet + 800 retrieved tokens, $200/mo infra, 100k req/mo
    c_rag = rag_cost_per_request(
        SONNET,
        user_tokens=200,
        retrieved_tokens=800,
        completion_tokens=400,
        monthly_infra_usd=200,
        monthly_requests=100_000,
    )

    investment = 3_000  # $1k GPU training + $2k engineering
    n_star = break_even_requests(c_prompt, c_ft, investment)

    print(f"Sonnet prompting cost/request : ${c_prompt*1000:.4f} per 1 000 req")
    print(f"Fine-tuned Haiku cost/request : ${c_ft*1000:.4f} per 1 000 req")
    print(f"RAG cost/request              : ${c_rag*1000:.4f} per 1 000 req")
    print(f"Saving per request (FT vs prompt): ${(c_prompt-c_ft)*1e6:.2f} per million req")
    print(f"Break-even volume             : {n_star:,.0f} requests")
    print(f"Break-even at 100k req/mo     : {n_star/100_000:.1f} months")
Running the above with default values:

Sonnet prompting cost/request : $12.0000 per 1 000 req
Fine-tuned Haiku cost/request : $1.6400 per 1 000 req
RAG cost/request              : $16.2000 per 1 000 req
Saving per request (FT vs prompt): $10,360.00 per million req
Break-even volume             : 289,575 requests
Break-even at 100k req/mo     : 2.9 months
Token spend is only part of the picture. Factor in every cost dimension before comparing approaches:
The following class encapsulates the full TCO model and generates a monthly projection. It accounts for the amortisation period you care about, infrastructure, and training spend:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
import math


@dataclass
class CostScenario:
    name: str
    pricing: TokenPricing
    input_tokens_per_req: int
    output_tokens_per_req: int
    monthly_infra_usd: float = 0.0
    one_time_usd: float = 0.0       # training + engineering
    amortise_months: int = 12

    def monthly_cost(self, monthly_requests: int) -> dict:
        token_cost = self.pricing.cost(
            self.input_tokens_per_req * monthly_requests,
            self.output_tokens_per_req * monthly_requests,
        )
        amortised = self.one_time_usd / self.amortise_months
        total = token_cost + self.monthly_infra_usd + amortised
        return {
            "scenario": self.name,
            "requests": monthly_requests,
            "token_usd": round(token_cost, 2),
            "infra_usd": round(self.monthly_infra_usd, 2),
            "amortised_usd": round(amortised, 2),
            "total_usd": round(total, 2),
            "cost_per_1k": round(total / monthly_requests * 1000, 4),
        }


def compare_scenarios(
    scenarios: list[CostScenario],
    monthly_requests: int,
) -> None:
    rows = [s.monthly_cost(monthly_requests) for s in scenarios]
    rows.sort(key=lambda r: r["total_usd"])
    header = f"{'Scenario':<22} {'Token$':>9} {'Infra$':>9} {'Amort$':>9} {'Total$':>10} {'$/1k':>8}"
    print(f"\n=== {monthly_requests:,} requests / month ===")
    print(header)
    print("-" * len(header))
    for r in rows:
        print(
            f"{r['scenario']:<22} "
            f"${r['token_usd']:>8,.2f} "
            f"${r['infra_usd']:>8,.2f} "
            f"${r['amortised_usd']:>8,.2f} "
            f"${r['total_usd']:>9,.2f} "
            f"${r['cost_per_1k']:>7.4f}"
        )


if __name__ == "__main__":
    scenarios = [
        CostScenario(
            name="Sonnet prompting",
            pricing=SONNET,
            input_tokens_per_req=800,   # 600-token system + 200 user
            output_tokens_per_req=400,
        ),
        CostScenario(
            name="RAG + Sonnet",
            pricing=SONNET,
            input_tokens_per_req=1_200, # +800 retrieved
            output_tokens_per_req=400,
            monthly_infra_usd=200,
        ),
        CostScenario(
            name="Fine-tuned Haiku",
            pricing=HAIKU,
            input_tokens_per_req=250,   # 50-token system + 200 user
            output_tokens_per_req=400,
            one_time_usd=3_000,
            amortise_months=12,
        ),
        CostScenario(
            name="FT Haiku + RAG",
            pricing=HAIKU,
            input_tokens_per_req=700,   # 50 system + 200 user + 450 retrieved
            output_tokens_per_req=400,
            monthly_infra_usd=200,
            one_time_usd=3_000,
            amortise_months=12,
        ),
    ]

    for n in [10_000, 100_000, 1_000_000]:
        compare_scenarios(scenarios, n)
Sample output at three volume tiers:

at low volume (< 30k req/mo) prompting wins because the fine-tuning investment dominates; above ~300k req/mo a fine-tuned smaller model is cheapest by a wide margin. RAG is most expensive per-request once the retrieval tokens are factored in — its advantage is quality (grounding), not cost.

The single biggest lever on prompting cost is the length of the system prompt. A verbose 2 000-token system prompt costs 2.5× more at the input tier than a 800-token one, and that multiplier compounds with every request. Fine-tuning eliminates the system prompt entirely for tasks where the behaviour is constant — the model's weights carry the instruction.

def prompt_length_sensitivity(
    pricing: TokenPricing,
    system_token_lengths: list[int],
    user_tokens: int,
    output_tokens: int,
    monthly_requests: int,
) -> None:
    print(f"\nPrompt length sensitivity — {monthly_requests:,} req/mo")
    print(f"{'System tokens':>15} {'$/req':>8} {'$/month':>10}")
    for sys_tok in system_token_lengths:
        cost_per_req = pricing.cost(sys_tok + user_tokens, output_tokens)
        cost_monthly = cost_per_req * monthly_requests
        print(f"{sys_tok:>15,}  ${cost_per_req*1000:>6.3f}  ${cost_monthly:>9,.0f}")


prompt_length_sensitivity(
    SONNET,
    system_token_lengths=[100, 400, 800, 1_500, 3_000],
    user_tokens=200,
    output_tokens=400,
    monthly_requests=500_000,
)

When choosing where to run a fine-tuned model, the trade-off is fixed infrastructure cost versus variable API cost. API fine-tuning (OpenAI, Together AI, Fireworks) has no infrastructure management overhead but a higher per-token price. Self-hosting (a single A100 80 GB on a cloud VM) costs roughly $2–3 / hr or ~$1 500/mo reserved, but serving 1M tokens / hr makes the per-token cost negligible.
Hidden Costs That Are Easy to Miss
1 000 high-quality instruction pairs typically cost $500–2 000 if outsourced to domain experts. This often exceeds the GPU training cost.
Building reliable automated evals (LLM-as-judge, benchmark suites) to decide whether a fine-tune actually improved quality takes 3–10 engineering days — a cost that repeats on every update cycle.
If underlying knowledge changes monthly (product catalogue, regulations), you re-pay training costs monthly. RAG avoids this by re-indexing instead of re-training.
A fine-tuned model sometimes loses general capability on tasks not covered by the training set. Catching this requires a broad eval suite, which takes time to build and run.

A fine-tuned model drifts silently when the input distribution shifts. Logging inference inputs and periodically sampling outputs for quality checks adds an ongoing operational cost that prompting workflows also carry but to a lesser degree.
As a practical starting point before doing detailed modelling:
use prompting. The engineering time for fine-tuning is not repaid.
model the break-even explicitly. Fine-tuning typically pays off in 2–6 months.
fine-tuning (or self-hosted inference) is almost always the cheaper option — the per-token saving compounds quickly.
RAG wins regardless of volume because re-indexing is orders of magnitude cheaper than re-training.
